name: Frontal bone
blurb: Forms the forehead, anterior cranial floor, and orbital roofs; articulates posteriorly with both parietals at the coronal suture.
pearl: Houses the frontal sinuses; supraorbital notch/foramen transmits the supraorbital nerve/vessels (V1). Persistent metopic suture can mimic a fracture.

name: Parietal bone
blurb: Paired bones forming the bulk of the cranial vault roof and sides; meet at the sagittal suture and join the frontal at the coronal suture.
pearl: The pterion (frontal-parietal-temporal-sphenoid junction) is the thinnest skull point; fracture can rupture the middle meningeal artery -> epidural hematoma.

name: Temporal bone
blurb: Paired bones at the lower lateral skull housing the middle/inner ear; squamous, petrous, tympanic, and mastoid parts plus the zygomatic process.
pearl: Petrous part holds the cochlea/vestibule; basilar fracture causes Battle sign (mastoid bruising) and CSF otorrhea. Facial nerve exits the stylomastoid foramen.

name: Occipital bone
blurb: Forms the back and base of the skull around the foramen magnum; articulates with C1 (atlas) via occipital condyles and with both parietals.
pearl: Foramen magnum transmits the medulla/spinal cord, vertebral arteries, and spinal roots of CN XI. Atlanto-occipital joint enables head nodding ('yes').

name: Sphenoid bone
blurb: Butterfly-shaped keystone of the cranial base; central body with greater/lesser wings and pterygoid processes, articulating with most skull bones.
pearl: Sella turcica houses the pituitary (transsphenoidal route). Optic canal (CN II), superior orbital fissure (III, IV, V1, VI), foramina rotundum/ovale/spinosum.

name: Ethmoid bone
blurb: Light, spongy midline bone between the orbits forming part of the nasal cavity roof, medial orbital walls, and the upper nasal septum.
pearl: Cribriform plate transmits olfactory nerves (CN I); fracture can shear them (anosmia) and cause CSF rhinorrhea. Crista galli anchors the falx cerebri.

name: Maxilla
blurb: Paired upper-jaw bones fused at the midline that form the upper teeth sockets, hard palate, orbital floor, and nasal aperture margins.
pearl: Infraorbital foramen transmits the infraorbital nerve (CN V2); the maxillary sinus is the largest paranasal sinus and roots of upper molars project into it.

name: Zygomatic bone
blurb: The cheekbone forming the lateral orbital wall and rim; joins the maxilla, frontal, sphenoid, and temporal bones (zygomatic arch).
pearl: Common site of the tripod (zygomaticomaxillary complex) fracture from lateral facial trauma; transmits zygomaticofacial and zygomaticotemporal nerves.

name: Nasal bone
blurb: Two small rectangular bones forming the bony bridge of the nose, articulating superiorly with the frontal bone and the maxillary frontal processes.
pearl: Most commonly fractured facial bone; distal nose is cartilage, so a 'broken nose' usually involves the nasal bones plus septal cartilage.

name: Lacrimal bone
blurb: The smallest, most fragile facial bone in the medial orbital wall; its groove helps form the fossa for the lacrimal sac.
pearl: Lacrimal groove + maxillary frontal process form the nasolacrimal canal draining tears to the inferior meatus; key landmark in dacryocystorhinostomy.

name: Palatine bone
blurb: L-shaped bone deep in the face whose horizontal plate forms the posterior hard palate and vertical plate the lateral nasal wall.
pearl: Greater palatine foramen transmits the greater palatine nerve/vessels — landmark for maxillary nerve block; forms part of pterygopalatine fossa.

name: Inferior nasal concha
blurb: A separate scroll-like bone on the lateral nasal wall, distinct from the superior/middle conchae which belong to the ethmoid.
pearl: Forms the inferior meatus, the drainage site of the nasolacrimal duct; hypertrophy is a frequent cause of nasal airway obstruction.

name: Vomer
blurb: A thin plough-shaped midline bone forming the posteroinferior part of the bony nasal septum, sitting on the maxilla and palatine bones.
pearl: With the ethmoid perpendicular plate it makes the bony septum; deviation here causes a deviated septum. Vomer = Latin for 'ploughshare.'

name: Mandible
blurb: The lower jaw and only movable skull bone; its condyle forms the temporomandibular joint with the temporal bone.
pearl: Inferior alveolar nerve (CN V3) enters the mandibular foramen and exits the mental foramen — target of the inferior alveolar nerve block in dentistry.

name: Malleus
blurb: Outermost, largest ear ossicle, hammer-shaped; its handle attaches to the tympanic membrane and its head articulates with the incus.
pearl: Tensor tympani (CN V3) inserts on the malleus handle to dampen loud sound; the handle ends at the umbo, the otoscopic origin of the cone of light.

name: Incus
blurb: Anvil-shaped middle ossicle bridging malleus and stapes; its short process anchors it while the long process connects to the stapes.
pearl: The long process is most vulnerable to ischemic necrosis; incus erosion is the commonest ossicular defect in chronic otitis/cholesteatoma.

name: Stapes
blurb: Smallest bone in the body, stirrup-shaped; its footplate sits in the oval window transmitting vibration to the inner ear.
pearl: Stapedius (CN VII) dampens the stapes; otosclerosis fixes the footplate causing conductive hearing loss, treated by stapedectomy.

name: Hyoid bone
blurb: U-shaped neck bone at the C3 level that articulates with no other bone, suspended by muscles and ligaments above the larynx.
pearl: Only bone with no bony articulation; fracture is a forensic hallmark of manual strangulation. Suprahyoid/infrahyoid muscles anchor it for swallowing.

name: Atlas (C1)
blurb: Ring-shaped first cervical vertebra with no body or spinous process; cradles the skull at the atlanto-occipital joint.
pearl: Jefferson fracture = burst of the C1 ring from axial load; the transverse ligament holds the dens against the anterior arch.

name: Axis (C2)
blurb: Second cervical vertebra bearing the dens (odontoid), the peg the atlas pivots around for head rotation.
pearl: Hangman's fracture = bilateral pars/pedicle fracture from hyperextension; odontoid (dens) fractures are common in elderly falls.

name: Cervical vertebrae (C3–C7)
blurb: Small typical neck vertebrae with bifid spinous processes and transverse foramina transmitting the vertebral arteries.
pearl: Vertebral artery runs through foramina transversaria of C6–C1 (skips C7); C7 is the vertebra prominens with a long non-bifid spine.

name: Thoracic vertebrae (T1–T12)
blurb: Twelve mid-spine vertebrae with costal facets on bodies and transverse processes that articulate with the ribs.
pearl: Heart-shaped body, long downward-sloping spines, and demifacets for rib heads; the only region articulating directly with ribs.

name: Lumbar vertebrae (L1–L5)
blurb: Five large weight-bearing lower-back vertebrae with kidney-shaped bodies and stout horizontal spinous processes.
pearl: Lumbar puncture done at L3–L4 or L4–L5 (below conus medullaris ~L1/L2); L4–L5 and L5–S1 are the most common disc herniation sites.

name: Sacrum
blurb: Triangular bone of five fused vertebrae forming the posterior pelvis; articulates with the ilia at the sacroiliac joints.
pearl: Sacral promontory is a key obstetric/pelvimetry landmark; S2 marks the dural sac end and the posterior superior iliac spines.

name: Coccyx
blurb: Small terminal tailbone of 3–5 fused rudimentary vertebrae hanging from the sacral apex via the sacrococcygeal joint.
pearl: Coccydynia follows falls/childbirth; the coccyx is an attachment point for pelvic floor muscles and the anococcygeal ligament.

name: Manubrium
blurb: Uppermost broad part of the sternum; bears the jugular notch and articulates with the clavicles and 1st ribs.
pearl: Joins the body at the sternal angle (of Louis) at the T4/T5 disc — landmark for rib 2, the aortic arch, and the tracheal bifurcation (carina).

name: Body of sternum
blurb: Long flat midline segment of the sternum; its costal notches articulate with the costal cartilages of ribs 2–7.
pearl: Overlies the heart and is the target of chest compressions in CPR; usable for bone marrow aspiration, though the posterior iliac crest is preferred.

name: Xiphoid process
blurb: Small cartilaginous-to-bony tip at the inferior sternum; marks the xiphisternal joint at the T9 level.
pearl: CPR hand placement sits just above it; can fracture and injure the liver or diaphragm; ossifies and fuses with the sternal body with age.

name: True ribs (1–7)
blurb: Upper seven ribs that attach directly to the sternum via their own costal cartilage (vertebrosternal ribs).
pearl: Rib 1 is shortest/most curved; its scalene tubercle separates grooves for the subclavian vein (front) and artery (back) — site of thoracic outlet syndrome.

name: False ribs (8–10)
blurb: Ribs 8–10 whose costal cartilages join the cartilage of the rib above rather than the sternum directly (vertebrochondral).
pearl: Their joined cartilages form the costal margin (subcostal angle); rib 10 is typically the lowest rib reaching the costal arch anteriorly.

name: Floating ribs (11–12)
blurb: Lowest two ribs with no anterior cartilage attachment; their tips end freely in the posterior abdominal wall musculature.
pearl: Lack a tubercle and articulate with only one vertebral body (single facet, no costotransverse joint); the rib 12 tip overlies the kidney.

name: Clavicle
blurb: S-shaped strut bone bridging the sternal manubrium to the scapular acromion, forming the only bony link of the arm to the axial skeleton.
pearl: Most commonly fractured bone in childhood; classic break at the junction of middle and lateral thirds, its weakest point.

name: Scapula
blurb: Flat triangular shoulder-blade on the posterior thorax; its glenoid cavity articulates with the humeral head at the shoulder joint.
pearl: Winged scapula = long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) injury paralyzing serratus anterior; tests as a protruding medial border.

name: Humerus
blurb: Long bone of the arm; its head meets the glenoid proximally and its trochlea/capitulum meet the ulna and radius at the elbow.
pearl: Midshaft fracture risks radial nerve injury (wrist drop); surgical neck fracture risks axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery.

name: Radius
blurb: Lateral (thumb-side) forearm bone; its head pivots at the elbow and its distal end forms most of the wrist joint with the scaphoid and lunate.
pearl: Colles fracture: distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement ('dinner-fork' deformity), classic FOOSH injury in older adults.

name: Ulna
blurb: Medial (pinky-side) forearm bone; its trochlear notch grips the humeral trochlea, making it the main bone of the elbow hinge.
pearl: The olecranon is the bony point of the elbow; the ulnar nerve runs in the groove behind the medial epicondyle ('funny bone').

name: Scaphoid
blurb: Boat-shaped lateral bone of the proximal carpal row; articulates with the radius and is the largest proximal-row carpal.
pearl: Most commonly fractured carpal; tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Retrograde blood supply risks avascular necrosis of the proximal pole.

name: Lunate
blurb: Crescent-moon-shaped carpal in the center of the proximal row, articulating with the radius above and the capitate below.
pearl: Most commonly dislocated carpal; lunate dislocation can compress the median nerve and cause acute carpal tunnel syndrome.

name: Triquetrum
blurb: Pyramidal carpal at the medial end of the proximal row; its palmar surface bears a facet for the pisiform.
pearl: Second most commonly fractured carpal, typically a dorsal chip avulsion; key part of the medial wrist column.

name: Pisiform
blurb: Pea-shaped sesamoid bone sitting on the palmar surface of the triquetrum, embedded in the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon.
pearl: It is a sesamoid within the FCU tendon and forms the medial border of Guyon's canal, which transmits the ulnar nerve and artery.

name: Trapezium
blurb: Lateral carpal of the distal row articulating with the first metacarpal to form the highly mobile saddle joint of the thumb.
pearl: Its saddle (carpometacarpal) joint of the thumb is a frequent site of osteoarthritis; the FCR tendon grooves its palmar tubercle.

name: Trapezoid
blurb: Small wedge-shaped carpal of the distal row, articulating mainly with the base of the second metacarpal.
pearl: Smallest bone of the distal carpal row and the least commonly fractured/dislocated carpal due to its protected, wedged position.

name: Capitate
blurb: Largest carpal bone, sitting centrally in the distal row with a rounded head that fits into the lunate and scaphoid.
pearl: Largest carpal and the first to ossify; central location makes it the keystone of the wrist and a pivot in carpal kinematics.

name: Hamate
blurb: Wedge-shaped medial carpal of the distal row with a palmar hook (hamulus), articulating with the 4th and 5th metacarpals.
pearl: Hook of hamate fractures occur in golfers/racquet sports; it forms the lateral wall of Guyon's canal and can injure the ulnar nerve.

name: Metacarpals (I-V)
blurb: Five miniature long bones of the palm, numbered thumb to little finger, linking the carpus to the proximal phalanges.
pearl: Boxer's fracture is a 5th metacarpal neck fracture from punching; the heads form the knuckles when the fist is clenched.

name: Proximal phalanges (hand)
blurb: The first row of finger bones, one per digit including the thumb, articulating with the metacarpal heads at the MCP joints.
pearl: Each digit has a proximal phalanx; only the thumb lacks a middle phalanx, giving the hand 14 phalanges total per side.

name: Middle phalanges (hand)
blurb: The intermediate finger bones present in digits 2-5, lying between the proximal and distal phalanges at the PIP joints.
pearl: Absent in the thumb; the central slip of the extensor expansion inserts on its base, and its rupture causes boutonniere deformity.

name: Distal phalanges (hand)
blurb: The fingertip bones of all five digits, bearing the flattened ungual tuberosity that supports the nail bed and pulp.
pearl: Avulsion of the terminal extensor tendon at its base causes mallet finger (drooping fingertip); FDP inserts on its palmar base.

name: Ilium
blurb: Largest, fan-shaped superior part of the hip bone; its body forms the upper acetabulum and the wing forms the iliac crest and fossa.
pearl: ASIS is the origin of sartorius and the lateral end of the inguinal ligament; the iliac crest is a key bone-marrow biopsy site.

name: Ischium
blurb: Posteroinferior part of the hip bone; its body forms the posteroinferior acetabulum and bears the ischial tuberosity and spine.
pearl: Ischial tuberosity bears weight when seated and anchors the hamstrings; ischial spine is the landmark for a pudendal nerve block.

name: Pubis
blurb: Anteromedial part of the hip bone; meets its partner at the pubic symphysis and forms the anteroinferior acetabulum.
pearl: Pubic tubercle anchors the medial inguinal ligament; a groin hernia above and medial to it is inguinal, below and lateral is femoral.

name: Femur
blurb: Longest, strongest bone in the body; head articulates with the acetabulum, condyles meet the tibia and patella at the knee.
pearl: Femoral neck fractures risk avascular necrosis of the head by disrupting the medial femoral circumflex artery's retinacular supply.

name: Patella
blurb: Largest sesamoid bone, embedded in the quadriceps/patellar tendon; glides in the femoral trochlear groove anterior to the knee.
pearl: It increases the quadriceps' extension lever arm; patellar reflex tests the L2-L4 (femoral nerve) spinal segments.

name: Tibia
blurb: Medial, weight-bearing leg bone; plateau articulates with the femur, distal end forms the medial malleolus of the ankle.
pearl: Most commonly fractured long bone and most common open fracture due to its subcutaneous anteromedial (shin) surface.

name: Fibula
blurb: Slender lateral leg bone; bears little weight, distal end forms the lateral malleolus and stabilizes the ankle mortise.
pearl: Common fibular nerve wraps the fibular neck - fracture there causes foot drop with loss of dorsiflexion and eversion.

name: Calcaneus
blurb: Largest tarsal bone forming the heel; articulates superiorly with the talus and anteriorly with the cuboid, anchors the Achilles tendon.
pearl: Most fractured tarsal, classically from a fall onto the heels; look for an associated lumbar compression fracture (Lover's leap).

name: Talus
blurb: Transmits body weight to the foot; sits in the ankle mortise between tibia, fibula, and calcaneus, with no muscle attachments.
pearl: Largely retrograde blood supply means neck fractures (aviator's astragalus) risk avascular necrosis of the body.

name: Navicular
blurb: Boat-shaped medial midfoot tarsal lying between the talus head and the three cuneiforms, in the medial longitudinal arch.
pearl: Its tuberosity is the insertion of tibialis posterior; an accessory navicular is a common cause of medial midfoot pain.

name: Cuboid
blurb: Lateral midfoot tarsal between the calcaneus and the 4th/5th metatarsals, forming part of the lateral longitudinal arch.
pearl: Its inferior groove transmits the fibularis (peroneus) longus tendon toward the medial cuneiform/first metatarsal.

name: Medial cuneiform
blurb: Largest wedge-shaped cuneiform; articulates with the navicular proximally and the 1st (and partly 2nd) metatarsal distally.
pearl: Receives the tibialis anterior insertion and the fibularis longus tendon, which converge to control the medial arch.

name: Intermediate cuneiform
blurb: Smallest cuneiform, recessed between the medial and lateral cuneiforms; articulates with the 2nd metatarsal base.
pearl: Its recessed position locks the 2nd metatarsal base into a keystone, the pivot point disrupted in Lisfranc injuries.

name: Lateral cuneiform
blurb: Middle-sized cuneiform between the intermediate cuneiform and cuboid; articulates with the 3rd metatarsal base.
pearl: Forms part of the transverse arch and helps stabilize the Lisfranc (tarsometatarsal) joint complex.

name: Metatarsals (I-V)
blurb: Five long bones of the forefoot, each with base, shaft, and head; bridge the tarsals proximally to the phalanges distally.
pearl: 5th metatarsal base avulsion (pseudo-Jones) vs Jones fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction, which heals poorly.

name: Proximal phalanges (foot)
blurb: First row of toe bones; articulate with metatarsal heads at the MTP joints and with middle phalanges (or distal in the hallux).
pearl: The great toe (hallux) has only two phalanges, so each foot has 14 phalanges total, not 15.

name: Middle phalanges (foot)
blurb: Short intermediate toe bones present in toes 2-5; sit between the proximal and distal phalanges at the PIP joints.
pearl: Absent in the hallux; flexor digitorum brevis inserts here, splitting to let flexor digitorum longus pass to the distal phalanx.

name: Distal phalanges (foot)
blurb: Terminal toe bones bearing the nails; each has a flattened distal tuberosity and forms the tip of each toe.
pearl: Distal phalanx of the hallux receives flexor hallucis longus and extensor hallucis longus, key for push-off in gait.
